name: Borzoi
description: The Borzoi was bred by Russian aristocracy as a coursing hound capable of chasing and capturing wolves on the open steppes of Russia, combining aristocratic elegance with extraordinary speed and the power to hold a wolf by the neck until the hunter arrived. Their long, silky coat flows dramatically when they run, and their tall, narrow, aerodynamic build is perfectly adapted for high-speed pursuit across open terrain. Borzoi are sensitive, intelligent dogs with an independent sight hound character that can make training challenging — they respond well to calm, patient handling and are particularly gentle with people they trust.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions_detail.description: PRA is a group of hereditary diseases causing progressive degeneration of the photoreceptor cells in the retina. It leads to night blindness first, followed by complete blindness. There is no treatment, but DNA tests exist for many forms.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: There is no effective treatment or cure for PRA. Management focuses on adapting the dog's environment to maintain quality of life as vision deteriorates.
prevention_tips: DNA test breeding stock before mating
Only breed clear or carrier-to-clear pairings

conditions_detail.name: Osteochondritis Dissecans (OCD)
conditions_detail.description: OCD is a developmental condition where cartilage in a joint fails to convert to bone properly, forming a flap that causes pain, joint effusion, and lameness. Commonly affects shoulder, elbow, stifle, and hock joints.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Surgical removal or fixation of the cartilage flap is usually required. Medical management with rest and NSAIDs may help mild cases but rarely resolves the lesion.
prevention_tips: Avoid over-supplementation with calcium in growing puppies
Moderate exercise in young large-breed dogs
